title: The 13 Principles of Agroecology
author_organization: FAO / HLPE (High Level Panel of Experts on Food Security and Nutrition)
purpose: Read this first. These are the 13 principles this directory uses as its alignment benchmark — recycling, input reduction, soil health, animal health, biodiversity, synergy, economic diversification, co-creation of knowledge, social values and diets, fairness, connectivity, land and natural resource governance, and participation. Every fund assessment in this directory refers back to these principles.
description: The consolidated set of 13 agroecological principles developed by the HLPE, the scientific advisory body of the UN Committee on World Food Security. The definitive reference for what agroecology means in policy, investment, and practice contexts globally.

title: Agroecology Infopool
purpose: Start here alongside the FAO 13 Principles. The Infopool is the richest single agroecology-specific resource available — it explains the principles, hosts the B-ACT, F-ACT, and ACE assessment tools, links to the Agroecology Investment Guide, and publishes key policy briefs. Biovision also co-founded Neycha, giving this resource direct relevance to how agroecology investment is practiced on the ground.
description: Biovision's comprehensive agroecology knowledge hub covering definitions and principles, associated concepts, assessment tools (ACT, F-ACT, B-ACT, ACE), key topics including the agroecological business case, national agroecology strategies, and climate change. Also includes key publications and events. Regularly updated.

title: Agroecology Investment Guide
author_organization: Biovision Foundation / TIFS (The Investment & Finance for Sustainability Initiative)
purpose: Read this when you want to move from understanding agroecology to actively investing in it. The guide walks through how to assess agroecological enterprises, includes real AEE showcases rated against the 13 principles (showing strengths and weaknesses honestly), and profiles investment funds. The showcases page is a useful source for identifying fund candidates beyond this directory.
description: A practitioner guide to investing in agroecological enterprises, produced by Biovision and TIFS. Includes a showcase section profiling AEEs from different value chain positions with explicit notes on their 'agroecology-ness', as well as an investment fund showcase and a tools and downloads section linking all major agroecology assessment tools.

title: B-ACT: Business Agroecology Criteria Tool
purpose: Use this when doing due diligence on a specific enterprise you are considering investing in. The most rigorous business-level agroecology assessment available — it rates a company or cooperative against all 13 FAO principles and generates a scored profile. This is the tool Neycha uses to screen its own borrowers. If a fund claims its portfolio companies are agroecological, B-ACT is how you verify that claim.
description: The Business Agroecology Criteria Tool assesses an enterprise's alignment with the 13 principles of agroecology across its operations, supply chain, and governance. It is designed for investors, fund managers, and enterprises themselves. More rigorous than ACE; use B-ACT for in-depth evaluation after initial screening.

title: ACE: Agroecology Check for Enterprises (Quick Assessment)
purpose: Use this for a fast first-pass screen before committing to a full B-ACT assessment. ACE is a lighter version of B-ACT — quicker to complete, less granular in output. Useful for scanning a pipeline of potential investments to decide which ones warrant deeper evaluation with B-ACT.
description: A simplified agroecology assessment for enterprises, covering the same 13-principle framework as B-ACT but designed for speed. Best used as a first filter in investment screening rather than as a definitive assessment. Developed by Biovision as part of the Agroecology Infopool tool suite.

title: F-ACT: Farm Level Agroecology Criteria Tool
purpose: Use this when evaluating agroecology at the farm level rather than the business level. Less directly relevant for most fund-level investors, but useful if you are lending directly to individual farmers or evaluating a cooperative where farm-level practices are the core of the value proposition.
description: The farm-level counterpart to B-ACT. Assesses an individual farm's practices against the 13 principles of agroecology. Designed for agronomists, farm-level lenders, and organizations working directly with smallholder farmers. Distinct from B-ACT, which assesses the enterprise as a whole including non-farm operations.

title: ACT: Agroecology Criteria Tool
purpose: Use this when assessing a programme, initiative, or donor portfolio — as opposed to a single enterprise or farm. Designed for programme designers and donors evaluating whether their work contributes to agroecological transformation at scale. Bridges the enterprise-level tools (B-ACT, ACE) and the portfolio-level tool (Agroecology Coalition Finance Tool).
description: The Agroecology Criteria Tool assesses initiatives and programmes against the 13 principles of agroecology. Occupies a middle ground between the farm/business-level tools (F-ACT, B-ACT) and the portfolio-level finance assessment tool. Used by donors and programme designers to evaluate whether funded interventions are genuinely agroecological.

title: Agroecology Finance Assessment and Tracking Tool (Online)
author_organization: Agroecology Coalition / Finance and Investment Working Group
purpose: Use this when assessing whether a fund's or donor's overall portfolio supports agroecological transformation. The portfolio-level complement to B-ACT. Where B-ACT asks 'is this enterprise agroecological?', this tool asks 'does this fund or programme move food systems toward agroecological transformation?' Requires login but the manual is freely downloadable.
description: The online tool developed by the Agroecology Coalition's Finance and Investment Working Group, built on a framework developed by 20 organizations including FAO, IPES-Food, IFAD, Biovision, and the European Commission. Allows donors and investors to upload investment information and assess their portfolios against the 13 principles. Aggregate reports are published periodically to track global investment trends in agroecology.

title: Systemic Investment Assessment (SIA)
author_organization: TIFS (The Initiative on Food and Agriculture Systems)
purpose: Use this when you want to understand not just whether an investment is agroecological but whether it contributes to food systems transformation systemically. More abstract and policy-oriented than the Biovision or Coalition tools — asks questions about power, governance, and systemic change rather than principle-by-principle compliance. Best suited for development finance institutions, large philanthropies, or policy-oriented investors.
description: TIFS's framework for assessing whether an investment contributes to systemic food systems transformation, beyond measuring alignment with agroecological principles. Takes a broader view of change-making, including questions about power dynamics, governance structures, and the enabling environment for transformation. Used as part of the Agroecology Investment Guide's analytical framework.

title: Agroecology Coalition
author_organization: Agroecology Coalition (hosted by Bioversity International / Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT)
purpose: Follow this to track the policy and institutional landscape around agroecology investment. The Coalition is where governments, UN agencies, and civil society coordinate on agroecology financing — its Finance and Investment Working Group produced the Finance Assessment Tool, and its annual Agroecology Donors Convening is the main gathering for institutional investors and donors working in this space. Not a capital deployer but the most important policy network for understanding how the enabling environment for agroecology investment is evolving.
description: A global coalition of more than 300 members including pioneering governments, international agencies, scientific groups, and civil society organizations. Operates through working groups on research, policy, finance, communications, implementation, and markets. Hosted by Bioversity International in Rome. Distinct from the Agroecology Fund: the Coalition coordinates policy and builds tools; the Fund deploys philanthropic capital.

title: Agroecology Fund (AEF)
purpose: Follow this to stay connected to the grassroots agroecology movement globally. The Fund's grantee network — 350+ organizations in 70 countries — is the most comprehensive map of where agroecology is actually practiced and funded at the community level. Their regional fund model (which seeded FINAPOP's broader ecosystem) is a direct precedent for the kinds of vehicles in this directory. Out of scope as an investment vehicle (they deploy grants), but essential as a network for sourcing candidates and understanding context.
description: A US-based multi-donor philanthropic fund and network that has supported more than 350 agroecology organizations in 70 countries since 2012. Operates regional funds, supports grassroots farmer organizations, and publishes resources on agroecology financing. Distinct from the Agroecology Coalition: the Fund deploys philanthropic capital to grassroots organizations; the Coalition coordinates governments and institutions on policy.

title: FAO Agroecology Knowledge Hub
author_organization: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO)
purpose: Use this as a searchable database when researching specific agroecology topics, regions, or initiatives. The broadest and most authoritative single repository of agroecology knowledge globally — articles, case studies, videos, and project profiles, constantly updated. Less useful for investment-specific research but essential for understanding what agroecology looks like in specific geographic or thematic contexts.
description: The UN FAO's comprehensive knowledge repository on agroecology, covering science, practice, and policy. Includes a searchable database of agroecology initiatives, publications, and case studies organized by topic, country, and content type. The institutional home of the 10 Elements and the reference framework for the 13 Principles.

title: Agroecology Europe
purpose: Follow this if your research focus includes European agroecology investments or policy. Agroecology Europe connects researchers, farmers, and practitioners across Europe and publishes accessible explainers on agroecology principles, case studies, and policy developments. More practitioner-oriented and accessible than the FAO Knowledge Hub; less policy-focused than the Agroecology Coalition.
description: A European association of researchers, educators, practitioners, and civil society organizations promoting agroecology through science, practice, and policy. Publishes accessible resources on agroecological principles and hosts events connecting European agroecology actors. Includes a useful principles explainer that maps the 13 HLPE principles with plain-language descriptions.

title: Agroecology Research-Action Collective (ARC)
purpose: Follow this for North American agroecology scholarship and movement connections, particularly on food sovereignty, land access, and farm justice. ARC is more academic and movement-oriented than the other networks listed here — less directly relevant to investment vehicles, but useful for understanding the political economy of agroecology in the North American context and for tracking emerging research on agroecology financing.
description: A collective of scholars working on farm justice, food sovereignty, and agroecology in North America, alongside social movements of farmers, workers, and land defenders. Produces policy analysis, rapid-response letters, and academic publications. Members have contributed to IPES-Food reports. Distinct from the UK-based Agroecology Research Collaboration (which is currently on hold).
